description: How to build interactive 3D data globes with globe.gl that render OFFLINE in the mChatAI+ preview and on the hub. globe.gl bundles three.js internally and is loaded as a single UMD script. The make-or-break gotcha is offline textures: never load an external earth image. ASCII only.

guidelines.rule: Load globe.gl with a classic UMD <script src> that exposes the global Globe (e.g. https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/globe.gl@2.32.0/dist/globe.gl.min.js) -- NEVER an ESM 'import'. globe.gl BUNDLES three.js internally, so do NOT also load three or a three importmap; that double-loads three and breaks. The platform vendors globe.gl offline automatically.

OFFLINE TEXTURE TRAP (most common failure): do NOT set .globeImageUrl(...) / .bumpImageUrl(...) / .backgroundImageUrl(...) to an external CDN image -- those 404 offline and on the hub, leaving a black globe. Instead render a SOLID, COLORED globe: pass .globeMaterial(new THREE.MeshPhongMaterial({ color: <swatch surface>, shininess: 8 })) and .showAtmosphere(true).atmosphereColor(<swatch accent>).atmosphereAltitude(0.18). For starfield, set .backgroundColor(<swatch background>) (a color, not an image).

Construct it on a sized container that fills the viewport: const g = Globe()(document.getElementById('globe')); then g.width(innerWidth).height(innerHeight); and update both on window resize. The container must have an explicit size or the canvas collapses to 0.

For GLOWING ARCS between places (the flagship look): g.arcsData(arcs).arcStartLat(d=>d.s[0]).arcStartLng(d=>d.s[1]).arcEndLat(d=>d.e[0]).arcEndLng(d=>d.e[1]).arcColor(()=>[<accent>, <accentWarm>]).arcDashLength(0.5).arcDashGap(0.3).arcDashAnimateTime(2000).arcStroke(0.6). Define the city/capital coordinates INLINE in the code (lat/lng literals) -- no external data fetch.

For COUNTRY POLYGONS / choropleth, load the vendored world dataset: <script src='./libs/world-countries.js'></script> sets window.WORLD_COUNTRIES (a GeoJSON FeatureCollection). Then g.polygonsData(window.WORLD_COUNTRIES.features).polygonCapColor(f=>...).polygonSideColor(()=> 'rgba(...)').polygonStrokeColor(()=> <stroke>).polygonAltitude(0.01) (raise on hover/selection for spikes). Color by a value mapped from inline data keyed by feature.properties.name.

For POINTS / spikes (population, events): g.pointsData(pts).pointLat(d=>d.lat).pointLng(d=>d.lng).pointColor(()=> <accent>).pointAltitude(d=>d.v).pointRadius(0.4). Or use .hexBinPointsData for density. Keep the point count modest (a few hundred) for 60fps.

Make it feel alive on first load: g.controls().autoRotate = true; g.controls().autoRotateSpeed = 0.6; and set an initial point of view g.pointOfView({ lat: 20, lng: 0, altitude: 2.2 }). OrbitControls (drag to rotate, pinch/scroll to zoom) work on desktop AND touch out of the box -- do not add pointer-lock.

Mobile: the container must fill the viewport and resize on orientationchange; OrbitControls already handle one-finger rotate + two-finger zoom. Clamp devicePixelRatio (globe.gl uses three's renderer; set g.rendererConfig({ antialias: true }) and rely on the platform DPR clamp). Reduce arc/point/polygon counts on coarse-pointer devices for 60fps.

Light the globe so it reads: globe.gl adds default lights, but for a colored (textureless) globe add an ambient + a directional light via g.lights([...]) or accept defaults and use MeshPhongMaterial so the atmosphere + shading give it form. A pure black unlit sphere means you forgot lighting or used a missing texture.

Ship a COMPLETE interactive globe on first load: a spinning colored Earth with visible arcs/points/countries immediately, a short legend/title in the swatch style, and hover tooltips (g.arcLabel / g.polygonLabel / g.pointLabel). Never an empty black canvas waiting for data.
